narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances to predict first-time success. The model assessed your profile— including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the bottom (1st decile, well below the typical 5th or 6th), an LSAT score of 22.5 (far under the average of 36.7), an undergrad GPA of 2.7 (lower than the usual 3.21), low family income bracket, and plans for full-time work—and predicted an 82% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try. This high failure risk is why your application was rejected: law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your undergrad GPA, in that order of influence. Your low Year 3 decile, landing you in the 1st percentile of your school's grades, had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model a sharp drop-off in performance that often foreshadows bar struggles. Your LSAT score reinforced this heavily, as its below-average level is a top predictor of exam difficulty for students in similar situations. Interestingly, your undergrad GPA pushed slightly in a positive direction, helping a bit toward a passing prediction since it's not drastically off the norm, but it wasn't enough to counter the other two. Compared to typical students who get approved, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles with LSATs above 35 and GPAs over 3.2, your academics stand out as the key gap, especially since full-time status is common and didn't sway things much. Your low family income also aligns with many applicants but didn't help offset the academic signals here. While this stings, especially as someone committed to full-time study, it's not the end—many in your position improve by retaking the LSAT for a stronger score, bolstering undergrad-level grades through post-bac programs, or targeting schools with bar passage support for lower-decile students. Consider auditing undergrad courses or bar prep resources to build that track record, and reapply after showing upward trends, as models like this reward demonstrated improvement. You've got the drive; channeling it into these targeted steps can shift your profile dramatically for the next round.
